heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: NICE guideline Reference number: NG106 Published: 12 September 2018 Last updated: 03 September 2025 Download guidance (PDF)

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: 1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure 1.2 Diagnosing heart failure 1.3 Giving information to people with heart failure 1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction 1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction 1.6 Treating heart failure in people with

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, stan

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: The specialist heart failure multidisciplinary team (MDT) should work in collaboration with the primary care team, and should include a: lead physician with subspecialty training in heart failure (usually a consultant cardiologist) who is responsible for making the clinical diagnosis specialist heart failure nurse healthcare professional with expertise in specialist prescribing for heart failure,

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: The specialist heart failure MDT should: diagnose heart failure give information to people with newly diagnosed heart failure (see the section on giving information to people with heart failure ) manage newly diagnosed, recently decompensated or advanced heart failure (New York Heart Association class III to IV) optimise treatment start new medicines that need specialist supervision continue to ma

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: The specialist heart failure MDT should directly involve, or refer people to, other services, including rehabilitation, services for older people and palliative care services, as needed. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: The primary care team should carry out the following, at all times, for people with heart failure, including during periods when the person is also receiving specialist heart failure care from the MDT: ensure effective communication links between different care settings and clinical services involved in the person's care lead a full review of the person's heart failure care, which may form part of

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: For recommendations on the diagnosis and management of acute heart failure, see NICE's guideline on acute heart failure .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Discharge people with heart failure from hospital when their clinical condition is stable, and a management plan is in place. Take into account the wishes of the person and their family or carers, and the level of care and support that can be provided in the community. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: The primary care team should take over routine management of heart failure as soon as it has been stabilised and its management optimised. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: The specialist heart failure MDT should write a summary for each person with heart failure that includes: diagnosis and aetiology medicines prescribed, monitoring of medicines, when medicines should be reviewed and any support the person needs to take the medicines functional abilities and any social care needs social circumstances, including carers' needs. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Use the summary as the basis of the person's care plan, which should include: plans for managing the person's heart failure, including follow-up care, rehabilitation and access to social care symptoms to look out for in case of deterioration a process for any subsequent access to the specialist heart failure MDT if needed contact details for a named healthcare coordinator (usually a specialist hea

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.1 Team working in the management of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Give a copy of the care plan to the person with heart failure, their family or carers if appropriate, and all health and social care professionals involved in their care. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Take a history and perform a clinical examination and tests to confirm the presence of heart failure. [2010]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Measure N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (NT‑proBNP) in people with suspected heart failure. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Because very high levels of NT‑proBNP carry a poor prognosis, refer people with suspected heart failure and an NT‑proBNP level more than 2,000 nanogram per litre (236 picomole per litre) urgently, to have specialist assessment and transthoracic echocardiography within 2 weeks. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people with suspected heart failure and an NT‑proBNP level between 400 and 2,000 nanogram per litre (47 to 236 pmol per litre) to have specialist assessment and transthoracic echocardiography within 6 weeks. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that: an NT‑proBNP level of less than 400 nanogram per litre (47 pmol per litre) in an untreated person makes a diagnosis of heart failure less likely the level of serum natriuretic peptide does not differentiate between heart failure with preserved, mildly reduced and reduced ejection fraction. [2018, amended 2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Review alternative causes for symptoms of heart failure in people with NT-proBNP levels of less than 400 nanogram per litre. If there is still concern that the symptoms might be related to heart failure, discuss with a physician with subspeciality training in heart failure. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that: obesity, African or African–Caribbean ethnic background, or treatment with the following can reduce levels of serum natriuretic peptides: a diuretic an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor or angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI) or angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) a beta-blocker a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) high levels of serum natriuretic

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Perform transthoracic echocardiography to exclude important valve disease, assess the systolic (and diastolic) function of the left ventricle and detect intracardiac shunts. See the section on referral for echocardiography and specialist assessment in NICE's guideline on heart valve disease . [2003, amended 2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Use high‑resolution equipment operated by someone trained to the relevant professional standards to perform transthoracic echocardiography. Do not allow the need and demand for these investigations to compromise quality. [2003, amended 2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Ensure that those reporting echocardiography are experienced in doing so. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Think about alternative methods of imaging the heart (for example, radionuclide angiography [multigated acquisition scanning], cardiac MRI or transoesophageal echocardiography) if a poor image is produced by transthoracic echocardiography. [2003, amended 2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Perform an ECG and consider the following tests to evaluate possible aggravating factors or alternative diagnoses: chest X-ray blood tests: renal function profile thyroid function profile liver function profile lipid profile glycosylated haemoglobin (HbA 1c ) full blood count urinalysis peak flow or spirometry. [2010, amended 2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Try to exclude other disorders that may present in a similar manner. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: When a diagnosis of heart failure has been made, assess severity, aetiology, precipitating factors, type of cardiac dysfunction and correctable causes. [2010]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
Heart failure caused by valve disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people with heart failure caused by valve disease for specialist assessment and advice regarding follow-up. See the section on referral for echocardiography and specialist assessment in NICE's guideline on heart valve disease . [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Review the basis for a historical diagnosis of heart failure and manage care in accordance with this guideline only if the diagnosis is confirmed with cardiac imaging. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If heart failure is still suspected, but an underlying cardiac abnormality has not been identified, then refer to the specialist heart failure team. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.3 Giving information to people with heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Discuss the person's prognosis in a sensitive, open and honest manner. Be frank about the uncertainty in predicting the course of their heart failure. Revisit this discussion as the person's condition evolves. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.3 Giving information to people with heart failure
First consultations for people with newly diagnosed heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: The specialist heart failure multidisciplinary team (MDT) should offer people with newly diagnosed heart failure an extended first consultation, followed by a second consultation to take place within 2 weeks if possible. At each consultation: discuss the person's diagnosis and prognosis explain heart failure terminology discuss treatments discuss the risk of sudden death, including any misconcepti

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: See recommendations 1.7.1 and 1.7.2 for guidance on how to introduce the medicines listed in recommendations 1.4.1, 1.4.3 and 1.4.4. See also the section on other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a beta-blocker, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) and a sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitor to people with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction . [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: For people on the maximum tolerated dose of each of the 4 medicines who continue to have symptoms of heart failure, consider switching the ACE inhibitor to an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI). [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Alternative treatment combinations if certain medicines are not tolerated
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction who have symptoms of intolerance to ACE inhibitors (other than angioedema), offer an ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA and SGLT2 inhibitor. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Alternative treatment combinations if certain medicines are not tolerated
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with angioedema after taking an ACE inhibitor, or who have symptoms of intolerance to ARNIs: offer a beta-blocker, MRA and SGLT2 inhibitor and consider an ARB. [2025] For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on treatment combinations for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: In people with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, assess iron status and check for anaemia with all of the following blood tests: transferrin saturation (TSAT) serum ferritin haemoglobin. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider iron sucrose, ferric carboxymaltose or ferric derisomaltose for people with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and haemoglobin of less than 150 g per litre if they have iron deficiency defined as: TSAT of less than 20% or serum ferritin of less than 100 nanogram per ml. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: If iron deficiency anaemia is identified, do not assume that it is related to the person's heart failure and think about investigating for alternative causes. [2025] For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on IV iron therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction . Full details of the

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Ivabradine is recommended as an option in NICE technology appraisal guidance for treating heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. For full details, see the guidance on ivabradine (TA267, 2012) .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: If ACE inhibitors, ARNIs and ARBs are not tolerated, seek specialist advice and consider hydralazine in combination with nitrate. [2010, amended 2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Seek specialist advice about whether to offer hydralazine in combination with nitrate (especially if the person is of African or Caribbean ethnicity and has moderate to severe heart failure [New York Heart Association class III/IV] with reduced ejection fraction). [2010]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: For recommendations on digoxin for people with atrial fibrillation, see the section on rate and rhythm control in NICE's guideline on atrial fibrillation .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer digoxin to people with worsening or severe heart failure with reduced ejection fraction despite optimised treatment combinations as detailed in recommendations 1.4.1 to 1.4.4 . Seek specialist advice before starting treatment. [2010, amended 2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.4 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Avoid verapamil, diltiazem and short-acting dihydropyridine agents in people with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. [2003, amended 2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: See recommendations 1.7.1 and 1.7.2 for guidance on how to introduce the medicines listed in recommendations 1.5.1 to 1.5.3. See also the section on other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a beta-blocker, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) and a sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitor for treating heart failure with mildly reduced ejection fraction. See also recommendation 1.5.3. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: For people who have symptoms of intolerance to ACE inhibitors, consider an angiotensin-receptor blocker (ARB), a beta-blocker, an MRA and an SGLT2 inhibitor. See also recommendation 1.5.3 [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: For SGLT2 inhibitors recommended as options in NICE technology appraisal guidance for treating heart failure with mildly reduced ejection fraction, see the guidance on: empagliflozin (TA929, 2023) dapagliflozin (TA902, 2023) . For a short explanation of why the committee made the 2025 recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on treatment combinations

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: See recommendations 1.7.1 and 1.7.2 for guidance on how to introduce the medicines listed in recommendations 1.5.4 and 1.5.5. See also the section on other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider an MRA and an sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitor for treating heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. See also recommendation 1.5.5. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.5 Treating people with newly diagnosed and pre-existing heart failure with mildly reduced or preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: For SGLT2 inhibitors recommended as options in NICE technology appraisal guidance for treating heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, see the guidance on: empagliflozin (TA929, 2023) dapagliflozin (TA902, 2023) . For a short explanation of why the committee made the 2025 recommendation and how it might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on treatment combinations for hea

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.6 Treating heart failure in people with chronic kidney disease
paragraph_or_text_span: If the person's eGFR is 45 ml per minute per 1.73 m 2 or less, consider lower starting doses and smaller dose increments for the medicine combinations covered by recommendations 1.4.1, 1.4.3, 1.4.4, and 1.5.1 to 1.5.5. [2018, amended 2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.6 Treating heart failure in people with chronic kidney disease
paragraph_or_text_span: If the person's eGFR is less than 30 ml per minute per 1.73 m 2 , the specialist heart failure multidisciplinary team (MDT) should consider liaising with a renal physician. [2018, amended 2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Use the person's medical history and findings from their clinical assessment, their frailty status, prognosis and preferences when deciding: which specific medicines and medicine combinations to use (see recommendations 1.4.1, 1.4.3, 1.4.4 and 1.5.1 to 1.5.5) the order and timing for introducing each medicine the initial dose of each medicine and any subsequent dose increments when and how to opti

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Primary care prescribers should consider seeking advice from a heart failure specialist before starting someone on an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI). [2025] For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on starting and monitoring medicine use . Full details of the evidence and the c

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
ACE inhibitors, ARNIs, ARBs and MRAs
paragraph_or_text_span: Before prescribing an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI), angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) or mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA), measure the person's renal function and electrolyte levels. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
ACE inhibitors, ARNIs, ARBs and MRAs
paragraph_or_text_span: If the person is taking an ACE inhibitor, ARNI, ARB or MRA, measure their renal function and electrolyte levels: 1 to 2 weeks after starting treatment 1 to 2 weeks after each dose increment every 3 to 6 months once the maximum tolerated dose has been established any time renal function may be compromised. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
ACE inhibitors, ARNIs, ARBs and MRAs
paragraph_or_text_span: If the person's serum creatinine level increases by more than 50% or their potassium concentration increases to more than 5.5 mmol per litre, follow local guidelines. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
ACE inhibitors, ARNIs, ARBs and MRAs
paragraph_or_text_span: For potassium binders recommended as options in NICE technology appraisal guidance for treating hyperkalaemia, see the guidance on: patiromer (TA623, 2020) sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (TA599, 2022) .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
ACE inhibitors, ARNIs, ARBs and MRAs
paragraph_or_text_span: Measure the person's blood pressure, or ask the person to measure their own blood pressure, before and after each dose increment. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
ACE inhibitors, ARNIs, ARBs and MRAs
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with symptoms of postural hypotension, measure blood pressure according to recommendation 1.1.5 in NICE's guideline on hypertension in adults . [2025] For a short explanation of why the committee made the 2025 recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on starting and monitoring medicine use . Full details of the evidence and the committee's

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not withhold treatment with a beta-blocker solely because of age or the presence of peripheral vascular disease, erectile dysfunction, diabetes, interstitial pulmonary disease or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. [2010]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Assess for heart rhythm, heart rate and conduction abnormalities using a 12-lead ECG before deciding whether to prescribe a beta-blocker. [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not offer a beta-blocker to people with second-degree or third-degree heart block who do not have a pacemaker or to people with bradycardia (that is, a heart rate of less than 50 beats per minute). [2025]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Assess heart rate and clinical status after each dose increment. [2010]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with symptoms and bradycardia, consider repeating a 12-lead ECG after each dose increment. [2025] For a short explanation of why the committee made the 2025 recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on starting and monitoring medicine use . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review A: medicines for h

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not routinely monitor serum digoxin concentrations. Be aware that a digoxin concentration measured within 8 to 12 hours of the last dose may be useful to confirm a clinical impression of toxicity or non-adherence. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Interpret the serum digoxin concentration in the clinical context as toxicity may occur even when the concentration is within the therapeutic range. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Monitor all people with heart failure. Provide: a clinical assessment of functional capacity, fluid status, cardiac rhythm (minimum of examining the pulse), cognitive status and nutritional status a review of medication, including need for changes and possible side effects an assessment of renal function iron status and haemoglobin measurement. Note: This is a minimum. Provide further monitoring f

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Provide more detailed monitoring if the person has significant comorbidity or if their condition has deteriorated since the previous review. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Determine the frequency of monitoring based on the person's clinical status and the stability of their condition. If the person's clinical condition or medication has changed, use a short timeframe for monitoring (days to every 2 weeks). For stable people with proven heart failure, monitor at least every 6 months. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with heart failure who want to be involved in monitoring their condition, provide sufficient education and support from their healthcare professional to enable this to happen, with clear guidance on what to do in the event of deterioration. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
People under 75 with normal renal function
paragraph_or_text_span: For people aged under 75 in specialist care settings with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) more than 60 ml per minute per 1.73 m 2 , consider measuring N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide as part of optimising treatment. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Use diuretics for the relief of congestive symptoms and fluid retention in people with heart failure and titrate (up and down) according to need using the lowest dose required. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Make the decision to prescribe amiodarone in consultation with a specialist. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Review the need to continue the amiodarone prescription at the 6‑monthly clinical review. [2003, amended 2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people taking amiodarone liver and thyroid function tests, and a review of side effects, as part of their routine 6‑monthly clinical review. [2003, amended 2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: For people with heart failure and atrial fibrillation, follow the recommendations on anticoagulation in the section on stroke prevention in NICE's guideline on atrial fibrillation . Be aware of the effects of impaired renal and liver function on anticoagulant therapies. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: In people with heart failure in sinus rhythm, consider anticoagulation for those with a history of thromboembolism, left ventricular aneurysm or intracardiac thrombus. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with heart failure an annual vaccination against influenza. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with heart failure vaccination against pneumococcal disease (only required once). [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: For women, trans men and non-binary people of childbearing potential with heart failure, discuss contraception and pregnancy. If pregnancy is being contemplated or occurs, seek specialist advice. Subsequently, share specialist care between the cardiologist and obstetrician. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: See NICE's guideline on depression in adults with a chronic physical health problem .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not routinely advise people with heart failure to restrict their sodium or fluid consumption. Ask about salt and fluid consumption and, if needed, advise as follows: restricting fluids for people with dilutional hyponatraemia reducing intake for people with high levels of salt or fluid consumption. Continue to review the need to restrict salt or fluid. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people with heart failure to avoid salt substitutes that contain potassium. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: See NICE's guidance on smoking and tobacco and alcohol .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Advise that air travel will be possible for most people with heart failure, depending on their clinical condition at the time of travel. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
1.9 Other treatments and advice for all types of heart failure
paragraph_or_text_span: Ensure physicians are up to date with the latest Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) guidelines. Check the DVLA website for regular updates . [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not routinely offer coronary revascularisation to people with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and coronary artery disease. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider specialist referral for transplantation for people with severe refractory symptoms or refractory cardiogenic shock. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
Implantable cardioverter defibrillators and cardiac resynchronisation therapy
paragraph_or_text_span: Implantable cardioverter defibrillators and cardiac resynchronisation therapy are recommended as options in NICE technology appraisal guidance for treating heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. For full details, see the guidance on implantable cardioverter defibrillators and cardiac resynchronisation therapy (TA314, 2014) .

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
Implantable cardioverter defibrillators and cardiac resynchronisation therapy
paragraph_or_text_span: When discussing implantation of a cardioverter defibrillator: explain the risks, benefits and consequences of cardioverter defibrillator implantation, following the principles on shared decision making in NICE's guideline on shared decision making ensure the person knows that the defibrillator function can be deactivated without affecting any cardiac resynchronisation or pacing, and reactivated la

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
Implantable cardioverter defibrillators and cardiac resynchronisation therapy
paragraph_or_text_span: Review the benefits and potential harms of a cardioverter defibrillator remaining active in a person with heart failure: at each 6‑monthly review of their heart failure care whenever their care goals change as part of advance care planning if it is thought they are nearing the end of life. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with heart failure a personalised, exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation programme. The programme: should be preceded by an assessment to ensure that it is suitable for the person should be provided in a format and setting (at home, in the community or in the hospital) that is easily accessible for the person should include a psychological and educational component may be incorporated

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not offer long-term home oxygen therapy for advanced heart failure. Be aware that long-term home oxygen therapy may be offered for comorbidities, such as for some people with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (see the section on oxygen in NICE's guideline on chronic obstructive pulmonary disease in over 16s ). [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not use prognostic risk tools to determine whether to refer a person with heart failure to palliative care services. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If the symptoms of a person with heart failure are worsening despite optimal specialist treatment, discuss their palliative care needs with the specialist heart failure multidisciplinary team (MDT) and think about a needs assessment for palliative care. [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with heart failure and their families or carers access to professionals with palliative care skills within the heart failure team. [2003]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If it is thought that a person may be entering the last 2 to 3 days of life, follow NICE's guideline on care of dying adults in the last days of life . [2018]

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Note: Heart failure is, by definition, symptomatic.

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Heart failure with left ventricular ejection fraction of 50% or more, plus a structural issue in the heart including 2 or more of the following: left atrial volume greater than 34 ml per m 2 in sinus rhythm, or greater than 40 ml per m 2 in atrial fibrillation ratio between early mitral inflow velocity and mitral annular early diastolic velocity (E:e' ratio) greater than 11 left ventricular hypert

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
Heart failure with mildly reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Heart failure with left ventricular ejection fraction between 41% and 49%.

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
paragraph_or_text_span: Heart failure with left ventricular ejection fraction of 40% or less.

heading_path: Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and management
paragraph_or_text_span: A medicine that antagonises the action of aldosterone at mineralocorticoid receptors.
